sentences.text: My wife stayed all the time in Stutthof
She was-- when the Russians came, and they had to leave Stutthof, they were put on a ship.
And with this ship, went to-- through the Baltic Sea to the west.
And the guards were terrible guards.
They didn't give them soft water, you know.
Water-- they had to drink from the sea.
My wife finally-- they left.
People went to this ship, found-- I don't know how many-- 30, 40 Jewish woman, 50.
I don't know how many.
No.
My wife came.
She was sick.
She was taken to the hospital in a Eckernforde, German hospital.
She was diagnosed.
She has TB of the lungs.
TB of the lungs, with the people in the concentration camp, was-- right away you're pronounced dead-- pronounced dead-- dead.
Nobody survived under [? their ?] conditions of the [? war ?] [? resistance. ?] And TB-- at the end of July, she passed away.
When she died, she was around 30 years of age.
In Berlin?
You see-- [LAUGHS] you have to-- we are-- we are in the Russian zone.
We are under the Russians.
Now, I have to go to the-- have to go to the west-- to the west.
I have to reach either American zone or the English zone.
We are in Berlin, surrounded by the Russians.
But Berlin had three zones or four zones-- Russian zone, English zone, French zone, and US-- United States zone.
So we came to the east zone.
We smuggled ourselves by-- by [? throwing ?] to the west
With Dr.
Kronzon.
He already is married.
He has a wife, also a doctor.
He found a-- a relative-- we'll say relative-- a nephew, and we went to the American zone.
There  the United Nations Relief Organization takes over, took us over-- very fine, relatively fine conditions, clean rooms, good food, you know, et cetera.
And we're to wait-- to wait for a-- for a truck which will bring us to the west zone.
And it was approximately one week we had to wait, and then two or three trucks full with either Jews or with Poles or with others who want to go to the west side.
And we reported to Hanover.
It's a city already in western Germany.
I had to go to Eckernforde.
That is north-- north of Germany.
My friend, Kronzon says, I'm going south to-- to Munich, because he knew that there is accumulation of Jewish, which was seen in Munich.
And-- and we parted.
I found that my wife is dead.
I went also to Munich.
In Munich, I found many friends who were in a.
[INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I couldn't stand the life there in St.
Ottilien It was a [?
Jew ?] hospital-- hospital where they--
What?
St.
Ottilien.
It is a big monastery.
The Germans converted this monastery into a military hospital.
And after the Americans came and occupied, they going to give this hospital to the Jews to bring your-- your-- your sick, et cetera.
I worked [? there, ?] [? too, ?] yes.
Not very long, because the supply of doctors was relatively enough, and I went to Munich.
I went to Munich.
I went to the University Hospital of Munich, to the obstetrical department asking permission to come to see.
But I got very, very-- right away you can [? come. ?] After all, now I am a liberated Jew in Germany.
It's a completely different situation.
After three months I am already a full-time employee in this hospital.
And soon, I am already employed with a patient.
And I became a part of this hospital, where I spent five years.
From this hospital, I published 11 scientific papers.
On what subject?

Eight or nine were [? object ?] all connected with concentration camp-related effects, and then the related-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all from this-- from this-- the-- I knew that I have not enough material.
I have only 1,500 cases.
I've only around 80-- 80 malformed very high-risk abnormalities.
Of the children.
Yeah, babies born.
Most frequent was mongoloids.
Correct.
Mostly mongoloids.
And the other is what are called central nervous system.
Central nervous system.
Hydrocephalus, a lot of water; anencephelus, without the brain; microcephaly, small brain; spina bifida, the division of the spine.
To--
--severe hunger and emotional stress of the mother, which affected her ovaries, because they were liberated.
Please, I am talking about women who are already over a year liberated.
You couldn't divide, but being in concentration camp, knowing what most affected, I said, it is not hunger.
It is emotion stress which kills more people than hunger.
Sure, you know.
Through damage-- damage of the ovaries, and damage of the ovaries, and damage of the gametes, which is in the ovaries.
My dear, my dear, it is simple.
It could-- could affect, directly, the ovaries.
If a woman is suddenly under severe emotional stress, she may stop menstruating.
She may stop menstruating, and that is, if you want to ask why, because the ovaries are affected.
The ovaries are not producing, working normally, and you can expect everything [? that you want ?] possible-- maybe miscarriages, maybe malformations, et cetera.
Nobody was talking.
I was talking  about this.
Sure-- kept alive.
I kept alive, and the Jewish committee even established a special place for all these malformed children.
I don't know.
I will not say where it was.
Dr.
Priskin, who was the director of all this told me, because I presented the results of these studies to-- at a convention of Jewish doctors.
All right, fine.
When was it?
It was somewhere beginning 1948.
What?
Yes, she could.
She could.
Nobody compelled her.
But you must understand that these women-- if a child had, we'll say, some other handicaps-- I will give you an example.
And nobody wants to stay in Germany.
Some are going to South Africa.
Some are going to Palestine.
Some are going to Canada, Australia-- Canada, a few.
The United States, too, was closed, until the Act of Displaced Persons came out, where they allowed 300,000 displaced persons to come to the United States.
But it is not Jewish.
It is mostly Poles, mostly Russians, mostly Czechs, all from the east countries.
We didn't want to go back-- back to-- to Russia.
That will not-- the Americans will not allow.
That was not allowed.
If you want in, either you live here, or you stay here.
Stay here.
Very many stayed with the children.
What?
My dear--
--they had their-- their doctors.
No, also from a liberated-- liberated.
One, for example, Doctor Herman-- he was a very good surgeon, but he worked at that time just checking all these things.
You know, people were going to the United States.
He had to check their health.
No, never.
Never.
Nobody-- nobody kills-- kills.
If you kill, you killed.
It is an old crime.
We are not allowed, as doctors, to kill abnormal children.
I worked at the University Hospital in Munich for four or five years.
I published a lot of scientific papers, all connected, mostly connected, with the concentration camp with a very strong response from other countries, even the Germans, and whatever.
Finally, the Russians published their result, all confirming my findings.

I introduced that it is not nor usually acceptable to do [INAUDIBLE] if you damage an embryo already-- or you-- if you deliver, you didn't have-- naturally, a surgeon, it could be that you have inherited such a thing.
I introduced the term that it is plasmatic damage of the egg, that the plasma of the egg is damaged.
I couldn't define what it is.
I could define, but it is the plasma of the egg is damaged.
Yes.
The-- the nucleus and the-- that was damaged.
Hunger and severe emotional distress.
You cannot relate to this, but it is hunger and severe emotional distress.
It's accepted all over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- the-- hold on.
Four years after I published my papers, a paper appears in Sweden, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where they studied, now, the cells-- ovarian cells, we'll say-- cells, the gametes.
First, they said it is not 48 chromosomes, but 46 chromosomes.
And then they segregated in a very fine way-- these two America scientists, Tjio and Levan, they did these studies in Sweden at the University of Sweden.
Found?
They didn't find.
[INAUDIBLE] They found that-- they first went into the how a cell is built.
We had-- we knew already it is 48 chromosomes, but they-- they did a beautiful job.
For me, they deserve to have a Nobel Prize.
And now, you know, when this was established, now, in England, they started to study hormonal deficiencies in the babies.
Maybe it is connected with the chromosomal status.
And the first [? full ?] did it on-- because most frequent-- in my papers was most frequent-- were mongoloids.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Lejeune is the name of a scientist from-- in Paris.
He started to study the chromosomes in mongoloids.
And here he found, you know, that instead of 46 chromosomes, they have 45 chromosomes.
One chromosome is missing.
Sure, with just one child.
I married a woman-- a woman who I got acquainted with in the concentration camp.
Galina Raicinska.
Oh, sure after my wife.
It was three-- it was almost four years after she died.
No, no-- three years after my wife died.
Boy.
Anatol.
In the University Hospital where I worked.
Yes.
My dear, I will-- I will give you, if you want, I will give you a wider answer.
All right.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I came to my professor, who was very good to me, I will say.
And I told him, Herr professor, I decided I am leaving Germany.
He was a 72-year-old, 73-year-old man.
I am already 88, so [INAUDIBLE] I considered him very old.
He jumps up in German, and he says,
I said, I know I will have here very well.
My son probably will have it well, too.
But you killed, in the 11th century, Jews because of Crusades.
Whole towns-- towns were destroyed by the crusaders when they were going [INAUDIBLE].
In the 13th and 14th centuries, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews were killed.
In Europe, was terrible epidemics, accusing that the Jews brought it.
Again, the Jews were killed.
In the 15th century, already gone were the epidemics.
Germany is divided into small principalities, barons and graf, and a Jewish merchant will come to sell his merchandise and pay taxes.
And such a baron or graf will say, why I have to-- to take taxes?
I will kill him, and then I will take the whole merchandise.
And that was, I say, in the 15th century.
Then for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 30 years, Protestants were fighting the Catholics. 1618, 1614.
And then still, you know, I said to him German, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And ?] Jewish [INAUDIBLE].
And now, what I went through here.
Ergo, you are a country here of physical anti-Semites.
Ergo ?] [INAUDIBLE] to come here back.
Not one Jew, in my opinion, has to stay in Germany.
Neither in Germany, nor in Poland, nor in Hungary, nor in Romania-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all countries with physical anti-Semites.
He looked at me and didn't say a word.
We remained best friends for 12 years.
He came even with his wife to New York.

He stayed in my apartment for almost two weeks, although he had to go to his daughter, who was married to an American doctor-- she's a doctor, and the American doctor-- in and out of town, Washington, Pennsylvania.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Whenever I was quite frequently going to Europe, every two years, making a trip.
I was making-- I made very soon a career, you know, here in the United States, working at Mt.
Sinai, having and developing a nice practice.
In obstetrics and gynecology.
I already applied-- I didn't have to take-- for specialty, now.
[INAUDIBLE] I had to get my specialty.
I was permitted to take-- because of my publications of my previous studies in Munich, I was-- didn't have to go as a resident, just simply to take the examinations.
And in 1960 I protracted.
And in 1960 I became a diplomat, American-style.
How long-- how long did you practice obstetrics and gynecology in New York?
Until what year?
[INAUDIBLE] life, I was in New York-- all [? the ?] life.
It is from '53 to '70-- to '80.
[INAUDIBLE].
So if I were to ask you now, how many babies did you personally deliver?
Approximately, I told you.
Approximately, it must be-- it stretches-- stretches around 40 years or 50 years of practice, you know.
So I counted [INAUDIBLE] approximately it should be around 7,000.
Mine-- I delivered.
But I was-- in [INAUDIBLE], I was the director.
I was before head of obstetrics for a year in the hospital, University Hospital.
I delivered there Jewish women.
Maternity ?] [? duty ?] other, you know-- it doesn't matter.
Over 3,000 under my supervision.
I am the responsible person.
And then, you know, together it would be around 20,000 under my supervision.
No.
No.
[? why am I ?] [? attached? ?] You know, I see this baby for, basically, a delivery, maybe one day later on, you know.
And that's it.
Now, proud?
Yes.
It was a very good specialty until malpractice started.
The most vulnerable specialty, which became in danger, very much, it was obstetrics.
And I said, you know, that in the 15th, 16th, 17th century, a woman gave birth to an abnormal child, and if the husband wanted to get rid of her, he would say she had relations with the devil.
That was enough.
She would be burned at stake.
Now, the new devil is not the devil.
It's the doctor.
When every woman delivers an abnormal child, the doctor's responsible.
And I said when I saw this, and I was hit with this, I said, that's enough.
This nonsense I don't want to put up with anymore.
And I'm sorry.
It was on my 80th birthday on November 1, 1887--
I retired.
In New York.
It is not-- 50 years.
Yes.
My second wife.
My second wife, shortly, will say, in '67, she was officially 49, but I know that she made herself younger, because after liberation she didn't have any papers.
She could say, I'm born on this day and this day.
And she's 53-- got a severe heart attack.
In 10 minutes.
In New York, on West End Avenue.
Two-- two sons.
Andrew Mark Klebanow.
He was born in America at Mt.
Sinai where I work.
That was a very, very big problem.
I was having-- I had at that time an excellent practice.
I had to bring-- you know, I had to-- it was a problem.
One son was 17 years old.
The other was 13 years old.
So it was not babies.
They were already grown up, that to manage them, yes, it was a hard time.
One was a failed help, that was the sister of my late wife.
No.
And then, you know, simply-- simply I had the women, you know, working at home [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] every day.
So [? dishonest, ?] they would go to school, and from school, you know, I am still keeping an eye on them.
They didn't need another.
And in 1970, I married my second wife-- my third wife.
What?
Sophie.
